channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: A City Hall Reporter Reveals Where the Scoops Come From
item.description: Sally Goldenberg covers New York City politics and government for The Times, attending to a network of sources more than 20 years in the making.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Juan Arredondo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sally Goldenberg, a former New York City Hall reporter at Politico, covers the administration of Mayor Zohran Mamdani for The Times.

item.title: At State Dinner, King Charles Charms the Court of Trump
item.description: King Charles III presented President Trump with a golden bell. “Should you ever need to get hold of us,” the king said, “well, just give us a ring!”
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: King Charles III at the state dinner at the White House on Tuesday night, delivering a speech with the right ingredients for President Trump's second term.

item.title: White House Urges House to Quickly Fund D.H.S.
item.description: The call amounted to a rebuke of Speaker Mike Johnson, who has delayed action on a homeland security spending bill and suggested this week that it needed changes.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: The memo calls for the “immediate passage” of a stalled bill to reopen the Department of Homeland Security.

item.title: Full Guest List for Trump’s State Dinner With Charles and Camilla
item.description: Six members of the Supreme Court were invited to the dinner, alongside top administration officials, billionaires and Republican lawmakers.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: President Trump and the first lady, Melania Trump, welcomed King Charles III of Britain and Queen Camilla to the White House on Tuesday evening for a state dinner.

item.title: See the Menu for the British Royal State Dinner
item.description: Spring-herbed ravioli and Dover sole meunière are among the dishes at the dinner honoring King Charles III of Britain and Queen Camilla.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: A dinner menu for the evening, seen at the White House.

item.title: Beneath King Charles’s Jokes and Decorum, Some Subtle Rebuttals to Trump
item.description: King Charles III gently pushed back against President Trump’s attacks on Britain and NATO, and he spoke of the importance of checks and balances.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: King Charles III addressing Congress on Tuesday.

item.title: Democrats Pitch ‘New Affordability,’ Looking to Widen Midterm Appeal
item.description: Progressives who expect their party to take control of Congress are putting forth a slate of bills aimed at helping lower costs for Americans, financed by tax increases on the wealthy.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Ilana Panich-Linsman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Greg Casar, Democrat of Texas, during a protest in Austin last year. Mr. Casar and the Congressional Progressive Caucus are trying to show voters that Democrats have their own policy solutions beyond simply dismantling President Trump’s agenda.

item.title: Trump’s Face Will Appear on Limited-Edition U.S. Passports, State Dept. Says
item.description: The department said it planned to release “a limited number of specially designed” U.S. passports that feature a picture of the president to commemorate the country’s 250th anniversary in July.
item.dc:creator: Taylor Robinson
item.media:description: An image of the proposed limited-edition passport design released by the State Department.

item.title: Donald W. Riegle, Representative Who Switched Parties, Dies at 88
item.description: Elected to Congress in 1966 as a Republican from Michigan, he grew disenchanted with his party during the Nixon presidency and became a progressive Democratic senator.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:description: Former Senator Donald W. Riegle during a news conference in his native Flint, Mich., in March 2016. Mr. Riegle endorsed Senator Bernie Sanders of Vermont, right, who was running for the Democratic presidential nomination against Hillary Clinton.

item.title: Treasury Issues More Sanctions on Iranian Oil Exports
item.description: The measures aim to crack down on Iran’s shadow banking system and Chinese purchases of Iranian oil.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: “Iran’s shadow banking system serves as a critical financial lifeline for its armed forces, enabling activities that disrupt global trade and fuel violence across the Middle East,” Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a statement.

item.title: Kennedy Center Works to Make the Case That Repairs Are Urgent
item.description: In Washington and in federal court, the center is arguing that its planned two-year closure is crucial. Critics say it’s a result of declining attendance and fleeing artists.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Zach Montague
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Among the changes the Trump administration has brought to the Kennedy Center is the addition of the president’s name to the exterior. Now it is slated to close for reasons that are the subject of debate.

item.title: Supreme Court Appears Skeptical of Falun Gong Lawsuit Against Cisco
item.description: The court’s decision could have broader implications for lawsuits seeking to hold companies liable for international human rights abuses.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Jonah Markowitz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of Falun Gong during a protest meditation in Manhattan in 2024. The Chinese government has banned the group.

item.title: Florida Republicans Refuse to Take Up DeSantis Bill Loosening Vaccine Mandates
item.description: The House speaker cited concerns about “children being in school without measles and mumps and polio and chickenpox vaccines that have been working for decades.”
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:description: Gov. Ron DeSantis of Florida wanted lawmakers to consider a bill that would allow more children to opt out of certain vaccines.

item.title: National Guard Can Stay in Memphis, State Appeals Court Says
item.description: The decision reversed a lower-court ruling last year that temporarily blocked the deployment.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:description: Gov. Bill Lee of Tennessee had sent National Guard members to Memphis to support a federal task force.

item.title: What to Know About the Kimmel vs. Trump Feud
item.description: The president and first lady took exception to a joke. Jimmy Kimmel defended it.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jimmy Kimmel said of a line that annoyed the president: “It obviously was a joke.”

item.title: Appeals Court Rules Against Trump Detention Policy, Creating a Split
item.description: The question of whether undocumented immigrants can settle in the United States without facing arrest and detention is likely headed to the Supreme Court.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Delaney Hall, an Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention center in Newark, N.J., last month.

item.title: DOJ Secures New James Comey Indictment Over Alleged Threat Against Trump
item.description: The new case stems from a social media post showing seashells on a North Carolina beach that the Trump administration characterized as a threat against the president.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett and Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Monica Jorge for The New York Times
item.media:description: James B. Comey, the former F.B.I. director, was indicted last year by a grand jury in Virginia, accused of lying and obstructing a congressional investigation over testimony he gave in 2020. The charge was later dismissed.

item.title: Trump Tests the Limits of His Most Faithful Supporters
item.description: The president’s rhetoric on religion, along with hard-line immigration policies and the U.S. war in Iran, has splintered a coalition of Christian voters who returned him to the White House.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Donald J. Trump at the National Prayer Breakfast in February.

item.title: After Another Attempt on Trump’s Life, Is Political Violence on the Rise in the U.S.?
item.description: A conversation with Sean Westwood, a professor at Dartmouth who tracks political violence and Americans’ perceptions of it.
item.dc:creator: Sabrina Tavernise
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Law enforcement officers at the scene after gunshots were fired during the White House Correspondents’ Association Dinner at the Washington Hilton on Saturday.

item.title: Military Quietly Accelerates Boat Strikes, Deploying More Aircraft
item.description: The military has increased the number of secret fixed-wing attack aircraft and armed MQ-9 Reaper drones, according to two people familiar with the matter.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:description: A ground crew member inspecting a MQ-9 Reaper drone in Aguadilla, P.R., last year. The military has increased the number of drones operating from Puerto Rico and El Salvador recently.

item.title: G.M.’s Profit Bolstered by a $500 Million Tariff Refund
item.description: Because of a February Supreme Court decision, General Motors said it would receive refunds on some of the tariffs it had paid.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.media:credit: Brett Carlsen for The New York Times
item.media:description: A General Motors plant in Spring Hill, Tenn. The company is still affected by other tariffs — including those on imported steel, aluminum, cars and auto parts.

item.title: Civil Rights Cases Slow at Education Dept. Amid Trump’s Overhaul
item.description: Data obtained by The New York Times shows that the Education Department resolved 30 percent fewer discrimination complaints in 2025 compared with the previous year.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender
item.media:description: Linda McMahon, the education secretary, is scheduled to testify on Tuesday to a Senate appropriations subcommittee that controls her department’s budget.

item.title: George Clooney Calls for Unity After White House Correspondents’ Dinner Shooting
item.description: The actor, who was honored at Film at Lincoln Center’s Chaplin Award Gala, denounced political violence after the attempted assassination of President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Krista Schlueter for The New York Times

item.title: F.B.I. Knew Civil Rights Group Informants Helped Bring Down Extremists, Lawyers Say
item.description: The Southern Poverty Law Center is planning to challenge the larger story of deceit and hypocrisy the Trump administration has been telling about its use of paid informants, court papers suggest.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:description: Southern Poverty Law Center lawyers met with prosecutors two weeks before the Justice Department unsealed its indictment against the organization in an effort to persuade them that the informant program, which was closed three years ago, had not been used to fund hate groups, but to hold them accountable.

item.title: Despite Trump’s Demands, His Fed Pick Is Unlikely to Get a Quick Rate Cut
item.description: The Federal Reserve is expected to hold interest rates steady this week as Jerome H. Powell presides over what is likely to be his last meeting as chair.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:description: It is unclear whether Jerome H. Powell will leave the Federal Reserve once his successor as chair is confirmed. His term as governor runs until early 2028.

item.title: Republicans Brace for Brutal Midterms as Trump’s Popularity Slips
item.description: The elections are still six months off, and some within the G.O.P. say there is still time to right the ship.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:description: The Republican Party’s outlook has dimmed along with voter faith that President Trump can fix the economy.

item.title: Trump’s Top Child Care Official Wants a ‘Bonfire of Regulations’
item.description: As Americans face soaring child care costs, Alex Adams wants to loosen rules and tighten spending. Critics say that will harm children and shutter day cares.
item.dc:creator: Coral Davenport
item.media:credit: Illustration by Celina Pereira; source images by Bloomberg, Christoph Wagner, Star Tribune, via Getty Images
